hook: You already know how to describe data using the mean and standard deviation, and you've seen that many real-world datasets form a symmetric, bell-shaped curve. But knowing the shape isn't enough — what if you needed to find the probability that a randomly chosen person's height falls between 160 cm and 175 cm, or determine what score marks the top 10% of test-takers? Those questions require more than the mean and standard deviation alone. How do we use the structure of a normal distribution to calculate exact probabilities?
concept: A normal distribution is a symmetric, bell-shaped probability distribution completely described by its mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. Every normal distribution can be converted to the standard normal distribution (mean 0, standard deviation 1) using the z-score formula $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, and then a z-table (or calculator) gives the cumulative probability — the proportion of data at or below any value.
anatomy: The key components are: the mean $\mu$, which sets the center (peak) of the bell curve; the standard deviation $\sigma$, which controls the spread (larger $\sigma$ means a wider, flatter bell); the raw score $x$, a specific data value you are investigating; the z-score $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, which measures how many standard deviations $x$ lies above or below the mean; and the cumulative area (written $P(Z \leq z)$), which is the probability read directly from the standard normal table or a calculator function such as normalcdf.

worked_example.description_latex: We are told that SAT scores are approximately normally distributed with mean $\mu = 500$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 100$, and we want $P(X \leq 650)$. The first thing we do is identify the three given quantities so we can build the z-score — this converts our specific score into a universal standard we can look up.

We apply the z-score formula to transform $x = 650$ into standard units. A z-score of $1.5$ tells us that 650 is exactly 1.5 standard deviations above the mean, which lets us use the standard normal table regardless of the original scale of the data.

Looking up $z = 1.5$ in the standard normal table (or using a calculator's normalcdf function) gives a cumulative area of $0.9332$. This means approximately 93.32% of test-takers scored at or below 650.

We interpret the result in context: there is about a 93.3% probability that a randomly chosen test-taker scored 650 or below, which also means roughly 6.7% scored above 650. Always translate the decimal back into a meaningful statement about the original situation.

partial_example.description_latex: Scores on a class exam are normally distributed with mean $\mu = 70$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 8$. We want the probability of scoring between 60 and 80. Because this is a two-sided interval, we will need to convert both boundary values to z-scores and then subtract the smaller cumulative area from the larger one.

We compute both z-scores using $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$. Notice that 60 and 80 are symmetric around the mean, so their z-scores are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign — a useful check that our arithmetic is correct.

Using the standard normal table, look up $P(Z \leq 1.25)$ and $P(Z \leq -1.25)$, then subtract to find $P(-1.25 \leq Z \leq 1.25)$. What is the probability that a randomly chosen student scored between 60 and 80?

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A variable is normally distributed with $\mu = 50$ and $\sigma = 5$. Find the z-score for $x = 55$.
Heights of adult men are normally distributed with $\mu = 70$ inches and $\sigma = 3$ inches. Find $P(X \leq 73)$.
Using the same height distribution ($\mu = 70$, $\sigma = 3$), find $P(67 \leq X \leq 76)$.
A normal distribution has $\mu = 100$ and $\sigma = 15$. The top 5\% of values exceed what score? (Use $z = 1.645$ for the 95th percentile.)

A bottling machine fills bottles with a mean of $\mu = 16.02$ oz and $\sigma = 0.04$ oz. Quality control rejects bottles below 15.94 oz or above 16.10 oz. Approximately what percentage of bottles are accepted?

common_mistakes: Students subtract the z-scores from each other instead of subtracting the cumulative probabilities: for a two-sided interval you must find $P(Z \leq z_2) - P(Z \leq z_1)$, not $z_2 - z_1$. Always look up each z-score in the table first, then subtract the areas.

When finding the probability of being above a value (right tail), students use the table value directly instead of subtracting from 1. The standard normal table gives $P(Z \leq z)$, so $P(Z > z) = 1 - P(Z \leq z)$ — you must subtract from 1 for any right-tail question.

Students mix up the direction of the z-score formula by computing $\frac{\mu - x}{\sigma}$ instead of $\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, which flips the sign of the z-score and leads to the mirror-image probability. Always place the raw score $x$ in the numerator first, then subtract the mean.
untested_variants: Finding a raw score $x$ when given a percentile and working backwards through the z-score formula: $x = \mu + z\sigma$ (inverse normal / percent-to-value problems not covered above).
Using the empirical (68-95-99.7) rule to estimate probabilities at exactly one, two, or three standard deviations from the mean without a z-table.
Comparing two individuals from different normal distributions using their z-scores to determine who performed relatively better.
Problems involving the normal approximation to the binomial distribution, where a discrete binomial situation is modeled as a continuous normal distribution.
